title: Joining clay with score and slip
subtitle: The standard method for attaching clay pieces at the leather-hard stage
excerpt: Score-and-slip is how two pieces of clay are attached: crosshatch the surfaces to be joined, apply a thin layer of clay slurry, press firmly together, and blend the seam. Without scoring and slipping, dry-on-dry joins pull apart as the piece dries. The method works for attaching handles, assembling slab boxes, joining coils, and adding surface decoration.
sourceNotes: Score-and-slip joining is universal in hand-building pottery. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Clay does not stick to itself by pressure alone once it has begun to dry.
Score-and-slip
is the solution: scratching the surfaces opens them up, and the
slip

fills the scratches and forms a chemical bond between the two pieces. The technique is the same whether attaching a handle to a mug, assembling a slab box, adding a coil to a pot base, or pressing a sprig decoration onto a tile. No kiln is needed when working with air-dry or paper clay.

attrs.body: Both pieces should be at the same drying stage for the best join. Two leather-hard pieces joined well. Wet clay joined to leather-hard clay is more likely to crack as they dry at different rates: the wetter piece shrinks more as it dries.
attrs.title: Matching drying stages

content.text: What you need

items.name: Air-dry or paper clay pieces to join
Clay slip (clay mixed with water to thick cream)
Wooden rib or finger for blending

content.text: Making slip

Put a small amount of the same clay as your project into a jar or old yogurt pot. Add water gradually, mixing with a finger or stick until the clay is the consistency of thick double cream: it should coat the finger without running off immediately. Always use the same clay as the pieces you are joining: different clay bodies dry at different rates and shrink by different amounts.
The joining method
Step 1: Score both surfaces

Use the needle tool to scratch a crosshatch of lines across both surfaces that will come into contact, covering an area slightly larger than the join. Score firmly enough to leave visible lines, 1 to 2 mm deep. A fork held flat against the clay surface works as well as the needle tool and covers more area per stroke. Do not score surfaces that are bone dry: the scratches will not grip on dried clay.
Step 2: Apply slip to both surfaces

Apply a thin layer of slip to both scored surfaces with a finger or a small brush. The slip should cover the scored area completely and penetrate the crosshatch grooves. Too much slip is wasteful and can create air pockets: a thin even coat is enough. The scored surface should look wet all over.
Step 3: Press and hold

Press the two surfaces together firmly with your thumbs and hold for 10 to 15 seconds. A slight wiggle of one piece against the other while pressing helps the slip penetrate any remaining gaps. Do not slide the pieces across each other: sliding smears the slip off the scored area.
Step 4: Blend the seam

On the interior of the join, use a finger or wooden rib to smooth the seam into both pieces, working in short strokes from the join outward. This reinforces the join mechanically by adding a fillet of blended clay. On the exterior, smooth the seam flush so it is not visible. Wipe away any excess slip with the damp sponge before it dries.
What a good join looks like

A correctly joined seam should be invisible from the outside after smoothing. Press along the seam with a fingertip: the clay should feel continuous, not stepped or raised at the join line. On the interior wall, the blend should slope smoothly into both pieces with no gap. As the piece dries, the seam should stay closed: a good
leather-hard
join at matching moisture content rarely fails.
Common problems

attrs.intro: Problems with score-and-slip joins and what to do.

items.fix: Score and slip again at the earliest sign of cracking. Press a small coil of fresh clay into the crack on the inside, blend it in, and allow to dry slowly under plastic.
items.cause: The pieces were at different drying stages, or the join was not blended on the interior.
items.symptom: Join cracks open as the piece dries

items.fix: Score both surfaces deeply and use a thin, even slip layer. Holding the join for 20 seconds rather than 10 helps.
items.cause: Surfaces were not scored, or slip was applied too thickly and formed a skin rather than bonding.

items.fix: Allow the slip to thicken in the jar with the lid off for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it reaches thick cream consistency again.
items.cause: Too much water was added when making the slip.
items.symptom: Slip is too runny and drips down the pot

attrs.heading: Common problems
